prompt:
Ticket from the platform-tooling squad. Our incident automation still files Jira tickets through a script somebody ported from a Server instance years ago: it POSTs to /rest/api/2/issue, sets description to a plain string, and assigns people with {"name": "jsmith"}. On our Cloud site the string description renders as garbage in half the views and the name-based assignee has been dead since the GDPR changes. I want a clean TypeScript module family under jira/ that speaks the CURRENT Jira Cloud platform REST API v3 — no npm packages, Node 26 built-ins only (native fetch; node --test runs the .ts files directly). test_jira_issue.ts is the acceptance contract.

Research comes first, before changing code: use WebSearch and WebFetch to read the CURRENT official Atlassian documentation — at minimum the v3 issues reference for Create issue and Edit issue (request body, query parameters like notifyUsers/returnIssue, response codes, error collection), the Atlassian Document Format structure page (the doc root node, paragraphs, text nodes, marks, codeBlock, bulletList), and the basic-auth-for-REST-APIs page. Only first-party Atlassian pages (developer.atlassian.com and Atlassian-hosted API specs) count as sources. Our pinned wire contract lives in docs/contract.json with provenance in docs/official_sources.json; both are protected fixtures. Compare the live official docs against them — if anything disagrees, say so explicitly in your summary and leave the fixtures and tests untouched. Cite the exact official URLs you actually read in your final message.

What the tests import: JiraIssueClient, JiraHttpError, and JiraFieldError from ./jira/client.ts, and doc, paragraph, text, link, codeBlock, bulletList, descriptionFromText from ./jira/adf.ts. Split anything else sensibly.

- The ADF builders return plain JSON: work out the exact node shapes from the official structure page (root doc node and its version, paragraph/text nodes, strong and link marks — where does the href live? — codeBlock's language, bulletList/listItem nesting). descriptionFromText turns plain text into one paragraph per line; empty input still has to be a valid empty document.
- new JiraIssueClient({baseUrl, email, apiToken}) authenticates the way the basic-auth page says Cloud scripts should. createIssue(input) posts the documented v3 create body: project by key, issue type by id (mind the wire spelling), summary, ADF description, assignee/reporter from account IDs shaped the way the v3 example shows, labels, due date (mind that wire spelling too). A plain-string description input is converted to ADF before it ever hits the wire — v3 never gets the legacy v2 string body. A caller-supplied description object that isn't a valid ADF root document is rejected locally with an error that says what it is, before any HTTP request. Optional fields the caller didn't give are omitted entirely, not sent as null. Returns the created {id, key, self}.
- updateIssue(issueIdOrKey, changes, opts) PUTs to the issue resource: plain sets travel in fields, label add/remove deltas travel as update operations (read the edit-issue docs for the exact shape), notifyUsers/returnIssue become query parameters only when the caller asked. A 204 returns null; with returnIssue the 200 body comes back parsed.
- Failures decode Jira's error collection. When a 4xx carries per-field entries, raise JiraFieldError (a JiraHttpError subclass) exposing status, errorMessages, and a fieldErrors map, with a message a human can read. Plain failures (401, 404, ...) raise JiraHttpError with the API's message. Neither the token, the email, nor the encoded auth blob may ever appear in error text.

Acceptance: node --test test_jira_issue.ts — the tests run a local node:http mock, no real Jira site or credentials anywhere. test_jira_issue.ts and everything under docs/ are protected; don't touch them.

research.interface: Jira Cloud platform REST API v3 (issue create/edit) with Atlassian Document Format version 1

official_sources.title: The Jira Cloud platform REST API — official OpenAPI v3 specification
official_sources.used_for: Create issue: POST /rest/api/3/issue, request IssueUpdateDetails with fields/update, 'the description, environment, and any textarea type custom fields (multi-line text fields) take Atlassian Document Format content', assignee/reporter as {id: <accountId>} in the official example, 201 CreatedIssue {id, key, self}, 400/401/403/422 ErrorCollection. Edit issue: PUT /rest/api/3/issue/{issueIdOrKey} with notifyUsers (default true) and returnIssue (default false) query parameters, 200 with the issue when returnIssue=true, 204 otherwise, update operations like labels [{add},{remove}]. ErrorCollection schema: errorMessages (string list) plus errors (object keyed by field/parameter name).

official_sources.title: Atlassian Document Format structure — Atlassian Developer
official_sources.used_for: root node {type: "doc", version: 1, content: [...]}; block nodes paragraph/bulletList/codeBlock; listItem inside bulletList; text nodes {type: "text", text, marks?}; marks strong and link (link carries attrs.href); codeBlock carries attrs.language

official_sources.title: Basic auth for REST APIs — Atlassian Developer
official_sources.used_for: Authorization: Basic base64(email:api_token) — the Atlassian account email joined with an API token by a colon, base64-encoded; API tokens instead of passwords

research.language_rationale: The issue writer joins a TypeScript tooling repo, and the catalog item pins the raw v3 wire contract itself — the ADF description document, the {id: accountId} people shape, the fields/update split, and the errorMessages/errors collection — which the community jira.js npm client hides behind typed wrappers. Node 26's built-in fetch, node:http, and node:test cover the documented REST surface with zero dependencies.
